title: Absolute measurement of the intrinsic helicity in nanophotonics
authors: Malo Bézard, Simon Garrigou, Jérémie Béal, Andreas Horrer, Yves Auad, Hugo Lourenço-Martins, Davy Gérard, Mathieu Kociak
abstract: Helicity is a universal quantity describing the internal rotation of an object or a field. Whether it characterizes fundamental properties such as the spin of elementary particles or leads to practical consequences such as the toxicity or harmlessness of chiral molecules, defining and measuring it is essential. However, this remains ambiguous in the case of chiral photonic systems such as plasmonic nanoparticles or photonic metasurfaces, where contrary to common knowledge, the observation of circularly polarized dependent optical properties is not a relevant measure for the helicity. We demonstrate experimentally and theoretically that the helicity can be rigorously defined and measured in a nanophotonic system emitting circularly polarized light after excitation in the near-field by a focused electron beam. In the case of a model system composed of two plasmonic dipoles (Born-Kuhn systems), we show that the helicity of photonic modes takes on a very intuitive form and can be simply measured by symmetrizing the geometry of excitation and detection. The method could be extended to a variety of chiral photonic systems, whose locally enhanced properties make them promising for the engineering of local chirality.
summary: This paper provides a new method to unambiguously measure the intrinsic helicity of nanophotonic structures by summing dichroic signals from symmetric positions. It resolves the confusion between geometric chirality and experimental artifacts like 'hidden chirality' in achiral plasmonic dimers.
main_problem: The paper addresses the ambiguity in defining and measuring intrinsic helicity in nanophotonic systems, specifically distinguishing true geometric chirality from 'extrinsic' or 'hidden' chirality arising from experimental geometry and dipole interference.
main_result: The authors establish a rigorous definition of plasmonic helicity and demonstrate that the intrinsic helicity can be measured by symmetrizing the excitation and detection geometry, effectively canceling out extrinsic effects.
method: The study combines theoretical vector analysis and a two-dipole model with experimental polarized cathodoluminescence (pCL) measurements using a STEM setup and numerical simulations (MNPBEM and pyGDM).
model_or_system: The physical system is a Born-Kuhn system (BKS) consisting of coupled gold nano-antennas (dimers) that can be configured in achiral or chiral geometries, excited by a focused free-electron beam.
key_observables: Circular dichroism (CDS) signal (g), Stokes parameter (S3), plasmonic helicity (Hpl), and electron energy loss spectroscopy (EELS) spectra.
important_parameters: Geometric chirality (K), optical chirality (C), dipole separation (d), antenna angle (alpha), and frequency (omega).
main_assumptions: The antenna separation is much smaller than the wavelength (k*d << 1), and the electron beam is assumed to excite only one dipole at a time due to field decay.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows achiral vs. chiral configurations and S3 distributions; Figure 2 details the BKS samples and pCL setup; Figure 3 presents spatially localized pCL maps; Figure 4 compares experimental and simulated helicity measurements; Supplemental figures show EELS spectra, mode maps, and mirror-induced polarization effects.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the problem of chirality ambiguity, introduces the theoretical framework for plasmonic helicity, presents the experimental pCL platform, demonstrates the symmetrization method to recover intrinsic signals, and concludes with a discussion on the universality of the approach.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1 Principle of the measurement on the helicity Hpl. (a) an achiral K = 0 or (a) a chiral K = 1 system (here, a plasmonic BKS structure, see (c)) is excited locally with an electron beam represented by a black arrow. Its angular-dependent CDS circular dichroic emission S3 is collected over the whole far-field sphere, and Hpl is equal to its integral. S3 is represented on the north and south hemispheres, as viewed along the y axis (see c). The schematics is obviously not to scale. The clear mirror parity symmetry between the north and south hemispheres in the achiral case leads to a null integrated CDS and Hpl = 0. This symmetry is absent for the chiral structure, thus leading to...
Fig. 3 Spatially localized circularly polarized CL. (a) From pCL spectral imaging, one can compute maps filtered at different energies, as exemplified in the specific case of a 90◦

Fig. 2 BKS samples and STEM-pCL set-up. (a) The light emitted from the a BKS sample under electron irradiation (cathodoluminescence), is collected by a parabolic mirror. HADF images of a 90◦and of a 270◦BKS are shown in (b) and (c). The two polarizations of the collimated beam are filtered with a quarter-wavelength phase plate (QWP) and a linearly polarizing beam splitter (Polarizer) to be sent to two different optical fibers. The left and right polarized spectra in (d) are then measured simultaneously and their difference (g) recorded.

Fig. 4 Helicity measurement. (a) Experimental and (b) simulated (pyGDM) sum of the dichroic CL spectra acquired at the two tips of the 90◦and 270◦BKS structures of Figure 3. (c) Experimental and (d) simulated variations of the dichroic signal as a function of the BKS angle α, filtered at the wavelengths of the bonding and antibonding mode summed on the two tips of the BKS. kHpl,d as defined for the coupled dipole model is overlaid on the simulated data (pyGDM).
